did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Transfer Learning (TL) approaches to detect and classify skin cancer (specifically melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and nevus) using images from the ISIC 2017 dataset. It presents simulation results including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores for binary classification models implemented in Keras.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Transfer Learning (TL) models for skin cancer detection using the ISIC 2017 dataset. It includes specific methodology (pre-processing, architecture details like 17-layer CNN and ResNet/VGG19), simulation results with performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score), and a conclusion summarizing the proposed solutions. Although it contains a literature review section, its primary contribution is the implementation and testing of new AI models, classifying it as a research paper.

title: Detection of Skin Cancer using Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning Concepts
journal: 2022 IEEE 4th International Conference on Cybernetics, Cognition and Machine Learning Applications (ICCCMLA)
brief_summary: This study presents the detection and classification of skin cancer, specifically distinguishing melanoma from benign seborrheic keratosis and nevus, using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Transfer Learning (TL). The researchers utilized the ISIC 2017 dataset, which contained over 2,000 images, and implemented models using the Keras framework. The methodology involved pre-processing steps such as illumination correction, Gaussian filtering, and data augmentation through cropping and rotation. Two primary approaches were evaluated: a custom 17-layer CNN and pre-trained architectures like ResNet and VGG19. The models were assessed using binary classification metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, with the custom CNN achieving a test accuracy of approximately 80-86% depending on the optimizer and dropout settings. The authors conclude that while the proposed AI methods show promise for early skin cancer detection, further hyperparameter tuning and advanced pre-processing are needed to improve performance.

paper_terms: malignant skin tumour produced from melanocytes
lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin cancer; one of the primary classes in the ISIC 2017 dataset used (374 images in training set); distinguished from benign tumors.
Benign skin tumor; one of the primary classes in the ISIC 2017 dataset used (254 images in training set); grouped with nevus as 'non-melanoma' in binary classification tasks.

paper_terms: benign skin tumour, derived from keratinocytes
lesion_types_studied.details: Benign skin tumor; one of the primary classes in the ISIC 2017 dataset used (1372 images in training set); grouped with seborrheic keratosis as 'non-melanoma' in binary classification tasks.
Composite class formed by combining seborrheic keratosis and nevus for binary classification tasks (melanoma vs. non-melanoma).

all_task_types.target: Binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) and Multiclass classification (melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states that the study presents two solutions: a binary classifier distinguishing melanoma from non-melanoma (grouping seborrheic keratosis and nevus) and a multiclass classifier separating the three distinct classes. The primary task evaluated using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score is binary classification. The authors note that an initial attempt at multiclass classification failed, leading to the adoption of the binary approach as the primary method.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A dataset supplied by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) during their 2017 melanoma identification competition. It consists of dermoscopic skin images.

use.training: Used for training custom 17-layer CNN and Transfer Learning models (ResNet, VGG19). The training set was augmented via cropping and rotation to expand the dataset.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (specifically mentioned for InceptionV3 loss/accuracy in simulation results).
use.testing: Used for testing the final models to evaluate accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores.
use.other: The validation and test sets were provided by ISIC in addition to the training dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results were evaluated using performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

Transfer Learning Models (ResNet/VGG19/I.notes: The paper states that the final solution (likely referring to the TL approach or a specific configuration) achieved a 0.90 F1 Score on one of the evaluated models. It also notes that the pure CNN and TL solutions performed admirably, with the final one having a slight advantage due to its more intricate architecture. Specific metric breakdowns for ResNet and VGG19 are not provided in the text, only a reference to Figure 7 for InceptionV3 loss/accuracy.

ISIC 2017 Dataset.notes: An initial attempt at multiclass classification (separating melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and nevus) failed, leading to the adoption of the binary classification approach. The custom CNN achieved a testing error of 0.189, sensitivity of 14.86%, and specificity of 98% in a separate context mentioned in the literature review section (referencing prior work or a different configuration not fully detailed in Table I). The authors note that results fall short of state-of-the-art due to time constraints on hyperparameter tuning and pre-processing.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer Learning models used pre-trained architectures (ResNet, VGG19, InceptionV3) retrained on the ISIC 2017 dataset. The custom CNN was trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images were cropped and resized, but specific dimensions are not provided).

conclusions: The study successfully demonstrated the use of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Transfer Learning (TL) approaches to detect and classify skin cancer, specifically distinguishing melanoma from benign seborrheic keratosis and nevus.

An initial attempt at multiclass classification (separating melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and nevus) failed, leading the authors to conclude that a binary classification strategy (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) is more viable for this task.
The proposed Transfer Learning solution achieved a slight performance advantage over the pure CNN approach, with one evaluated model reaching an F1 Score of 0.90.

The custom 17-layer CNN model achieved a test accuracy of approximately 80-86% depending on the optimizer and dropout settings, with specific configurations yielding precision and recall scores around 0.70-0.80.
The authors acknowledge that the results fall short of state-of-the-art performance due to time constraints that limited extensive hyperparameter tuning and advanced pre-processing of the dataset.
Future work should focus on improving pre-processing techniques and hyperparameter optimization to enhance model accuracy and sensitivity.
The study highlights that while AI can assist in diagnosis, skilled dermatologists remain essential for final diagnosis and treatment decisions.
important_remarks: The paper acknowledges that the initial multiclass classification attempt (separating melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, and nevus) failed, forcing a shift to binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma).
Performance is limited by time constraints that prevented extensive hyperparameter tuning and advanced pre-processing, resulting in results that fall short of state-of-the-art benchmarks.

The custom 17-layer CNN achieved a test accuracy of 0.80-0.86 depending on dropout settings, with one configuration yielding a sensitivity of 14.86% and specificity of 98% (referenced in literature review context), indicating potential class imbalance or threshold issues.
Transfer Learning models (ResNet, VGG19, InceptionV3) showed slight performance advantages over the pure CNN, with one model achieving an F1 Score of 0.90.
The study relies on the ISIC 2017 dataset, which contains a small number of low-quality images, and does not report external validation on independent datasets.
The authors emphasize that AI serves as an assistive tool and that skilled dermatologists remain essential for final diagnosis and treatment decisions.
